state_snapshot.user_query: Assess the likely AIE mechanism for this molecule.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: External close-family evidence for cyano-stilbene/triarylvinyl scaffolds points to RIR-mediated AIE with planar/near-planar ICT (or LE/ICT-mixed) emitting states; internal baseline also shows lowest vertical state is bright (f≈0.29) consistent with an emissive delocalized/ICT-mixed state at the sampled geometry.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Rotational freedom exists (4 rotors), so post-excitation twisting cannot be excluded purely internally; however, verifier evidence indicates TICT is much less typical for alkoxy/aryl donors and is not commonly reported as the dominant dark state in close-family cyano-stilbene/triarylvinyl AIE systems.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Verifier notes many close-family AIE emitters can be described as LE or planar ICT; internal data lack quantitative CT metrics, leaving room that the bright S1 is primarily LE π–π* with only modest CT.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Mechanism space is now constrained by internal vertical excitation brightness plus external close-family precedent; remaining uncertainty is mainly ICT vs (less likely) TICT vs LE/ICT-mixed character.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-capable intramolecular H-bonded proton donor/acceptor motif (methoxy and nitrile only); no verifier evidence suggesting ESIPT.

state_snapshot.verifier_supplement_summary: No exact-identity hit; close-family cyano-stilbene/triarylvinyl AIE literature supports RIR with planar/near-planar ICT (or LE/ICT-mixed) emission and provides generic ICT-vs-TICT discriminators plus donor-type constraints that make TICT less likely for alkoxy/aryl donors.
state_snapshot.closure_justification_summary: Internal baseline shows lowest vertical state is bright; verifier close-family precedent for cyano-stilbene/triarylvinyl AIE systems favors planar ICT/LE-ICT emission under RIR rather than dark TICT, and donor-type constraints (alkoxy vs amino) further reduce TICT plausibility. Together sufficient to resolve ICT vs TICT in favor of ICT.
state_snapshot.pairwise_task_rationale: Internal attempt to obtain CT metrics to discriminate ICT vs TICT; key CT metrics unavailable from baseline parse route, so external verifier evidence was used to complete discrimination.
state_snapshot.pairwise_resolution_summary: Internal baseline shows lowest vertical state is bright; verifier close-family precedent for cyano-stilbene/triarylvinyl AIE systems favors planar ICT/LE-ICT emission under RIR rather than dark TICT, and donor-type constraints (alkoxy vs amino) further reduce TICT plausibility. Together sufficient to resolve ICT vs TICT in favor of ICT.

hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Stays substantial because 4 rotatable bonds and aryl–vinyl junctions can enable twisting that may create a low-oscillator-strength CT state quenched in solution and suppressed in aggregates.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Falls sharply because there is no proton donor/acceptor pair capable of an intramolecular excited-state proton transfer cycle.
hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Remains moderate because emission could be largely locally excited with AIE dominated by RIR even if CT is weak; needs S1 characterization to confirm.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Nonzero because mechanism labels cannot be cleanly separated without computed S1 brightness/CT character and any experimental environment-dependence data.

hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Downweighted because baseline electronic observables reduce overall underdetermination; remaining uncertainty is specific (ICT vs TICT) rather than global unknown.

hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Slightly downweighted as core observables exist; uncertainty is specific rather than fully open-ended.

planner_diagnosis_history: Task: assess the likely AIE mechanism (choose among ICT, TICT, ESIPT, neutral aromatic, unknown) for COc1ccc(/C(=C(/C#N)c2ccccc2)c2ccccc2)cc1 (C22H17NO). Current top1=ICT, runner-up=TICT. The structure is a donor–acceptor triarylethylene-like system (anisole donor, nitrile acceptor) with several rotatable bonds, so AIE could arise from restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) that enhances radiative decay; whether the emissive state is predominantly planar ICT/LE or a twisted CT (TICT) is uncertain. Top1 remains a working hypothesis because we do not yet know the S1 nature (LE vs CT), oscillator strength, and whether twisting lowers S1 and quenches emission in solution. Evidence to separate ICT vs TICT later: (i) baseline S0 geometry + S1 vertical excitation characteristics (oscillator strength, transition character/CT extent), (ii) qualitative torsional propensity around donor–vinyl/aryl bonds (TICT tends to prefer near-orthogonal donor/acceptor fragments) and whether S1 is dark. Shared prepared structure is available (ready; 10 conformers; selected conformer_id=2; 4 rotatable bonds). Macro can provide chemical-heuristic mechanism cues (D–A motif, RIR likelihood, ESIPT plausibility) but cannot compute excited-state properties. Microscopic can run one bounded baseline S0/S1 computation on the shared structure to obtain first-pass excited-state descriptors; first round should gather both macro and microscopic evidence to quickly test whether S1 is CT-like and whether it is bright (supporting ICT/LE) or potentially dark/twist-prone (supporting TICT).

Top1=ICT (strengthened). Top2=TICT (still plausible but weakened). Latest evidence: (i) Macro scan shows a clear donor–acceptor conjugated scaffold and high planarity proxy (~0.899), supporting conjugation-enabled ICT/LE emission; (ii) Microscopic baseline shows the lowest S1 is already bright (first excitation 3.6776 eV with f=0.2876; 'lowest_state_is_bright'), which argues against a dominantly dark low-lying CT state at the vertical geometry (often associated with TICT minima). However, TICT remains viable because 4 rotatable bonds and conformational variability could allow relaxation to a twisted CT state after excitation, which is not probed by this vertical-only baseline. Key remaining discriminator (ICT vs TICT): whether the excited state gains strong charge-separation and becomes dark upon twisting/relaxation (TICT) versus remaining reasonably conjugated/bright (ICT/LE-mixed). Verifier supplementation is still missing; closure justification is still missing. Best next action is a single bounded microscopic task to extract CT/transition descriptors from the existing baseline bundle, directly targeting ICT-vs-TICT discrimination with internal evidence at low cost.

Round budget: 2/20 (19 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.50), Top2=TICT (0.27). Latest evidence leaves Top1 essentially unchanged: vertical-state brightness and strong D–A conjugation/planarity still support ICT/LE-mixed emissive character. However, the attempted internal discriminator (CT descriptor extraction) returned 'ct_localization_proxy: not_available' and did not provide electron–hole separation / transferred charge metrics, so it did not further strengthen ICT over TICT. Top2 (TICT) is not significantly weakened because the key discriminator is post-excitation twisting/relaxation and conformer dependence, which remain untested. A third candidate (neutral aromatic) is slightly upweighted because absent CT metrics, an LE-dominant bright π–π* state remains plausible. Key remaining discriminator (ICT vs TICT): whether low-energy excited-state relaxation along aryl–vinyl torsions accesses a twisted, more charge-separated and potentially dark state (TICT) versus remaining relatively planar and emissive (ICT/LE). Verifier supplementation is still missing; closure justification is still collecting and not yet discriminative because internal CT metrics were unavailable. Best next action now is external verifier supplementation targeted at ICT vs TICT for triaryl-alkene nitrile/anisolyl push–pull systems, since the planned internal CT-descriptor route stalled due to missing descriptors in the current bundle.

Top1=ICT (0.68) vs Top2=TICT (0.16). Verifier supplementation is sufficient and provides high information gain with close-family context: cyano-stilbene/triarylvinyl AIE systems are commonly explained by RIR with planar/near-planar ICT (or LE/ICT-mixed) emitting states, and TICT is structurally/empirically less typical for alkoxy/aryl donors compared to classic amino-donor TICT systems. This supports (rather than challenges) current Top1. Evidence is not an exact-identity hit, but is close-family + generic discriminator criteria; it materially improves the ICT vs TICT decision posture. Closure justification for ICT__vs__TICT can move from blocked to sufficient for a decisive mechanism label under the fixed label space, with the remaining caveat that LE vs ICT-mixed is not fully separable from current internal CT-metric limitations.

macro_reports.reasoning_summary:
From the shared context, the molecule is a tri-aryl substituted vinyl system bearing a cyano group and an anisole (methoxyphenyl) ring: COc1ccc(/C(=C(/C#N)c2ccccc2)c2ccccc2)cc1. This provides (i) an electron-donating methoxy aryl fragment and (ii) an electron-withdrawing nitrile substituent connected through an extended conjugated C=C linker with multiple phenyl rings, which is consistent with an intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) capable donor–acceptor conjugated scaffold. Geometry proxies indicate high planarity (planarity_proxy ~0.899) in the selected conformer, supporting effective conjugation and thus a potentially bright, delocalized ICT/LE mixed excited state. However, the topology also includes multiple rotatable bonds (rotatable_bond_count = 4; torsion_candidate_count = 4) around aryl–vinyl/aryl–sp2 connections, implying the system can access twisted conformations; this structural freedom keeps TICT-like (twisted CT) states plausible in solution depending on the torsional PES and barriers. Conformer dispersion proxy is reported as 1.0 across 10 conformers, consistent with nontrivial conformational variability, though the selected conformer remains relatively planar. Overall: strong donor–acceptor + conjugation indicators favor ICT propensity, while torsional degrees of freedom provide a structural route to twisting that could facilitate TICT under some conditions.
Capability limit note: Only shared prepared-structure metadata and deterministic topology/geometry-proxy analysis are used. No excited-state calculations, no solvent modeling, no barrier scans, and no aggregate-state simulation are performed; conclusions are limited to structural propensity indicators.

evidence_cards.source: Tang et al., Chem. Commun. 2001, and subsequent AIE reviews; also Luo et al., Chem. Commun. 2001, 1740
evidence_cards.title: Cyano-substituted triarylethylene (HPS and analogs) as AIE-active ICT emitters: restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) mechanism
evidence_cards.observation: 1,1,2,3,4,5-hexaphenylsilole and related triarylethylene/cyano-stilbene derivatives are reported as archetypal AIE fluorogens. In dilute solution, free rotation of aryl rotors around the central C=C bond quenches emission. In aggregates or viscous media, restricted intramolecular rotation (RIR) restores emission. The emitting state in these systems is described as a locally excited (LE) or planar ICT state, not a dark TICT state. Solvatochromic redshifts are moderate and consistent with ICT character rather than full charge separation.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study (anisyl-diphenyl-cyanoethylene) belongs to the triarylethylene/cyano-stilbene family. This card establishes the dominant mechanistic framework reported for such scaffolds: RIR-based AIE with an ICT emitting state, providing a baseline against which TICT claims must be evaluated.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: RIR-mediated AIE; emitting state is planar ICT, not twisted dark TICT
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution fluorescence quenching, aggregate emission enhancement, viscosity-dependent emission, moderate positive solvatochromism

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, S.J. et al.; also Rettig, W. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 1986, 25, 971; and Bhattacharyya, K. Acc. Chem. Res. 2003, 36, 95
evidence_cards.title: Experimental criteria distinguishing ICT from TICT: dual emission, viscosity dependence, and polarity-induced quenching patterns
evidence_cards.observation: TICT states are characterized by: (1) dual emission bands (LE + CT) in intermediate polarity solvents; (2) strong viscosity dependence where high viscosity increases CT emission intensity (because the twisted conformer is stabilized by restricted rotation); (3) a large Stokes shift with near-complete quenching in nonpolar solvents and recovery in polar solvents; (4) a dark or weakly emissive CT state that becomes emissive only when the twisted geometry is stabilized. ICT states, by contrast, show: (1) single emission band with positive solvatochromism; (2) moderate Stokes shifts; (3) emission that persists across polarity ranges; (4) no viscosity-induced switching of emission character. For TICT, the key structural requirement is a freely rotatable donor-acceptor bond with a low barrier to 90° twist.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card provides the experimental discriminators needed to resolve the ICT vs TICT question for the target molecule. The presence or absence of dual emission, the sign of viscosity dependence, and the polarity-quenching pattern are the key observables that distinguish the two mechanisms.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires freely rotatable donor-acceptor bond, dual emission, and viscosity-dependent CT emission; ICT shows single-band positive solvatochromism without viscosity switching
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence in solvents of varying polarity and viscosity; DMABN as canonical TICT example

evidence_cards.source: Lim, S.-J. et al. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2011, 133, 11132; also Yuan, W.Z. et al. J. Phys. Chem. C 2010, 114, 6090
evidence_cards.title: Cyano-stilbene and alpha-cyanostilbene derivatives: AIE via RIR with ICT character, not TICT dark state
evidence_cards.observation: Alpha-cyanostilbene and related donor-acceptor stilbene derivatives bearing cyano acceptors and aryl/alkoxy donors are reported to show AIE behavior attributed to restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR). The emitting state is described as having ICT character (donor-to-acceptor charge transfer in a planar or near-planar geometry). These compounds are weakly emissive in solution due to free rotation of aryl groups, and become strongly emissive in aggregates. No dual emission or TICT dark state is reported for this scaffold class. The cyano group acts as an electron acceptor in a push-pull ICT framework, and the emission redshifts with increasing solvent polarity in a manner consistent with ICT, not TICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card directly addresses the scaffold class of the target molecule (anisyl-diphenyl-cyanoethylene is a close analog of alpha-cyanostilbene derivatives). The reported mechanism for this family is ICT-based AIE via RIR, with no evidence of TICT dark states.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: AIE in cyano-stilbene/triarylvinyl systems proceeds via RIR with ICT emitting state; TICT dark state not reported for this scaffold
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Fluorescence in THF/water mixtures, solid-state emission, solvatochromism measurements

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, Z.R.; Rotkiewicz, K.; Rettig, W. Chem. Rev. 2003, 103, 3899
evidence_cards.title: Structural requirements for TICT formation: freely rotatable amine donors vs. rigid or non-amine donors in push-pull systems
evidence_cards.observation: The comprehensive review of TICT states establishes that TICT formation is strongly associated with freely rotatable amino donors (dialkylamino, diphenylamino) where the nitrogen lone pair can decouple from the pi system upon 90° rotation. Alkoxy donors (methoxy, as in anisole) are noted to have much weaker TICT propensity because the oxygen lone pair is less directional and the rotational barrier to full decoupling is higher. For systems with aryl donors (phenyl, anisyl) rather than amino donors, the TICT pathway is generally not competitive with ICT or LE emission. The review also notes that TICT requires a significant driving force (large energy gap between LE and CT states in polar solvents) and a low rotational barrier.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule has a methoxy (anisyl) donor, not an amino donor. This card provides mechanistic context for why TICT may be less accessible in this scaffold compared to canonical TICT systems (DMABN, Julolidine derivatives). The limitation of TICT to amino-donor systems is a key discriminator for the ICT vs TICT question.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT is strongly favored by freely rotatable amino donors; alkoxy/aryl donors have much lower TICT propensity due to weaker lone-pair directionality and higher rotational barriers
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Systematic comparison of TICT-active (DMABN, dimethylaminobenzonitrile analogs) vs TICT-inactive (methoxy, phenyl donor) push-pull systems across polarity and viscosity ranges

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, S.J. et al.; also Rettig, W. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 1986, 25, 971; and Bhattacharyya, K. Acc. Chem. Res. 2003, 36, 95
evidence_cards.title: Experimental criteria distinguishing ICT from TICT: dual emission, viscosity dependence, and polarity-induced quenching patterns
evidence_cards.observation: TICT states are characterized by: (1) dual emission bands (LE + CT) in intermediate polarity solvents; (2) strong viscosity dependence where high viscosity increases CT emission intensity (because the twisted conformer is stabilized by restricted rotation); (3) a large Stokes shift with near-complete quenching in nonpolar solvents and recovery in polar solvents; (4) a dark or weakly emissive CT state that becomes emissive only when the twisted geometry is stabilized. ICT states, by contrast, show: (1) single emission band with positive solvatochromism; (2) moderate Stokes shifts; (3) emission that persists across polarity ranges; (4) no viscosity-induced switching of emission character. For TICT, the key structural requirement is a freely rotatable donor-acceptor bond with a low barrier to 90° twist.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card provides the experimental discriminators needed to resolve the ICT vs TICT question for the target molecule. The presence or absence of dual emission, the sign of viscosity dependence, and the polarity-quenching pattern are the key observables that distinguish the two mechanisms.

evidence_cards.experimental_context: Steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence in solvents of varying polarity and viscosity; DMABN as canonical TICT example

evidence_cards.title: Cyano-stilbene and alpha-cyanostilbene derivatives: AIE via RIR with ICT character, not TICT dark state
evidence_cards.observation: Alpha-cyanostilbene and related donor-acceptor stilbene derivatives bearing cyano acceptors and aryl/alkoxy donors are reported to show AIE behavior attributed to restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR). The emitting state is described as having ICT character (donor-to-acceptor charge transfer in a planar or near-planar geometry). The cyano group acts as an electron acceptor in a push-pull ICT framework, and the emission redshifts with increasing solvent polarity in a manner consistent with ICT, not TICT.

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, Z.R.; Rotkiewicz, K.; Rettig, W. Chem. Rev. 2003, 103, 3899
evidence_cards.title: Structural requirements for TICT formation: freely rotatable amine donors vs. rigid or non-amine donors in push-pull systems
evidence_cards.observation: The comprehensive review of TICT states establishes that TICT formation is strongly associated with freely rotatable amino donors (dialkylamino, diphenylamino) where the nitrogen lone pair can decouple from the pi system upon 90° rotation. Alkoxy donors (methoxy, as in anisole) are noted to have much weaker TICT propensity because the oxygen lone pair is less directional and the rotational barrier to full decoupling is higher. For systems with aryl donors (phenyl, anisyl) rather than amino donors, the TICT pathway is generally not competitive with ICT or LE emission. The review also notes that TICT requires a significant driving force (large energy gap between LE and CT states in polar solvents) and a low rotational barrier.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule has a methoxy (anisyl) donor, not an amino donor. This card provides mechanistic context for why TICT may be less accessible in this scaffold compared to canonical TICT systems (DMABN, Julolidine derivatives). The limitation of TICT to amino-donor systems is a key discriminator for the ICT vs TICT question.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT is strongly favored by freely rotatable amino donors; alkoxy/aryl donors have much lower TICT propensity due to weaker lone-pair directionality and higher rotational barriers
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Systematic comparison of TICT-active (DMABN, dimethylaminobenzonitrile analogs) vs TICT-inactive (methoxy, phenyl donor) push-pull systems across polarity and viscosity ranges

This provides (i) an electron-donating methoxy aryl fragment and (ii) an electron-withdrawing nitrile substituent connected through an extended conjugated C=C linker with multiple phenyl rings, which is consistent with an intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) capable donor–acceptor conjugated scaffold. Geometry proxies indicate high planarity (planarity_proxy ~0.899) in the selected conformer, supporting effective conjugation and thus a potentially bright, delocalized ICT/LE mixed excited state. However, the topology also includes multiple rotatable bonds (rotatable_bond_count = 4; torsion_candidate_count = 4) around aryl–vinyl/aryl–sp2 connections, implying the system can access twisted conformations; this structural freedom keeps TICT-like (twisted CT) states plausible in solution depending on the torsional PES and barriers. Conformer dispersion proxy is reported as 1.0 across 10 conformers, consistent with nontrivial conformational variability, though the selected conformer remains relatively planar. Overall: strong donor–acceptor + conjugation indicators favor ICT propensity, while torsional degrees of freedom provide a structural route to twisting that could facilitate TICT under some conditions.

hypothesis_pool.rationale: External close-family evidence for cyano-stilbene/triarylvinyl scaffolds points to RIR-mediated AIE with planar/near-planar ICT (or LE/ICT-mixed) emitting states; internal baseline also shows lowest vertical state is bright (f≈0.29) consistent with an emissive delocalized/ICT-mixed state at the sampled geometry.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Rotational freedom exists (4 rotors), so post-excitation twisting cannot be excluded purely internally; however, verifier evidence indicates TICT is much less typical for alkoxy/aryl donors and is not commonly reported as the dominant dark state in close-family cyano-stilbene/triarylvinyl AIE systems.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Verifier notes many close-family AIE emitters can be described as LE or planar ICT; internal data lack quantitative CT metrics, leaving room that the bright S1 is primarily LE π–π* with only modest CT.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Mechanism space is now constrained by internal vertical excitation brightness plus external close-family precedent; remaining uncertainty is mainly ICT vs (less likely) TICT vs LE/ICT-mixed character.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-capable intramolecular H-bonded proton donor/acceptor motif (methoxy and nitrile only); no verifier evidence suggesting ESIPT.
